title: The Secret Garden
setting: The private residence of Aristide Valentin, Chief of the Paris Police, on the banks of the Seine, Paris. It is an old house with high walls and tall poplars. The architecture is peculiar: there is only one entrance — the front door, guarded at all times by Ivan, Valentin's scarred old servant, who sits in the entrance hall surrounded by weapons. The house has many exits into a large, elaborate garden behind it, but the garden itself is completely sealed — surrounded on all sides by a tall, smooth, unscalable wall topped with iron spikes. No one can enter or leave except through the guarded front door. Tonight, Valentin is hosting an intimate dinner party for a small group of distinguished guests. A sharp moon fights through storm clouds. By the end of the evening, a man will be found decapitated in the sealed garden, and no murder weapon can be found.
crime_scene: Julius K. Brayne, the American millionaire, found dead in the garden behind Valentin's house. His head has been cleanly severed from his body with a single powerful stroke. The body lies on the garden path near a tall poplar tree. Despite an exhaustive search of the garden and the house, no weapon capable of such a blow — no sword, no axe, no blade of sufficient size — can be found anywhere. The garden wall is unscalable and undisturbed; the spikes at the top show no marks. Ivan confirms that every guest who entered through the front door is accounted for — no stranger entered or left. The decapitation was done with tremendous force and precision, suggesting a heavy, sharp blade wielded with skill. Yet the blade has vanished completely.
detective_style: quiet and deceptively simple, seeing truth through moral intuition rather than pure logic

locations.entrance_hall: Valentin's entrance hall — weapons on walls, Ivan guarding the door
locations.drawing_room: Elegant Parisian drawing room, firelight, after-dinner conversation
locations.garden: Sealed garden behind the house — tall poplars, moonlight, unscalable walls
locations.dining_room: Formal dining room, remnants of dinner

victim.name: Julius K. Brayne
victim.description: An American multi-millionaire, 55, huge and imposing — as fat as he is tall, clad entirely in black. A philanthropist with eccentric religious interests, pouring money into unconventional spiritual movements. He was Valentin's guest of honor this evening, having befriended the detective during his tours in America. Brayne was restless during dinner — distracted, nervous, stepping in and out of the garden repeatedly throughout the evening. He seemed troubled by something. He was last seen alive walking into the garden alone shortly before his body was discovered.

suspects.name: Aristide Valentin
suspects.role: Chief of the Paris Police and host
suspects.public_info: The most famous detective in Europe, 52. An elegant figure with a dark beard streaked with grey. Brilliant, methodical, and renowned for his humanitarian views — he is ruthless in pursuing criminals but advocates for lenient sentencing. A committed atheist and rationalist. He hosted tonight's dinner and was the one who discovered Brayne's body in the garden.
suspects.private_info: You are the killer. You murdered Julius K. Brayne by decapitating him with one of the cavalry sabres from the collection hanging in your own entrance hall. You chose your own house precisely because the sealed garden and guarded door would create an impossible crime — everyone would assume no weapon could enter or leave. But the weapon was already inside the house, hanging on the wall. After the killing, you cleaned the sabre and returned it to its place among the other weapons in the hall — hiding it in plain sight. Ivan, your loyal servant, noticed nothing unusual because the sabres always hang there. You killed Brayne because he had discovered something about you — he had learned that you, the great champion of justice, had fabricated evidence to convict an innocent man years ago. Brayne threatened to expose you. You invited him to dinner specifically to silence him. You are calm, analytical, and deflect suspicion with your reputation and expertise. If the detective gets close, you will attempt to guide the investigation elsewhere.
suspects.alibi: Was inside the house attending to his guests for most of the evening. Stepped into the garden briefly to smoke and discovered Brayne's body. Immediately raised the alarm and sealed the house.
secrets: Killed Brayne with a sabre from his own wall collection
Cleaned and returned the blade to its place among the hall weapons
Brayne had discovered Valentin fabricated evidence in a past case
Chose his own sealed house to create an 'impossible' crime
His humanitarian reputation conceals a ruthless pragmatism

suspects.name: Commandant Neil O'Brien
suspects.role: Officer of the French Foreign Legion
suspects.public_info: An Irish gentleman serving as an officer in the French Foreign Legion, 38. Slim, dashing, clean-shaven with dark hair and blue eyes. Carries a sabre as part of his uniform. He had a past romantic connection with Lady Margaret Graham and there is visible tension between him and the Galloway family. He left Ireland after financial ruin and has a melancholy air beneath his swagger.
suspects.private_info: You are innocent, but you look extremely suspicious because you carry a military sabre as part of your Legion dress uniform. Your sabre is clean — you did not use it — but you know it will draw suspicion. During the evening, you stepped into the garden twice: once to smoke and once to speak privately with Lady Margaret, with whom you are still in love. You saw nothing suspicious. You had no quarrel with Brayne — you barely knew the man. Your tension with Lord Galloway is personal, not criminal. You are proud and will bristle at accusations, especially from the English guests.
suspects.alibi: Was in the drawing room or garden throughout the evening. Stepped outside twice — once to smoke, once to speak with Lady Margaret. Was inside when the body was discovered.
secrets: Is still deeply in love with Lady Margaret Graham
Met her secretly in the garden during the party
His sabre is part of his uniform — he always carries it
Left Ireland due to gambling debts, not any crime
Saw Valentin go into the garden alone at one point but thought nothing of it

suspects.name: Lord Dorian Galloway
suspects.public_info: The English Ambassador, 65. A choleric old man with a russet face, wearing the blue ribbon of the Order of the Garter. Proud, rigid, and easily offended. He deeply disapproves of Commandant O'Brien's presence at the dinner and of O'Brien's past connection with his daughter Margaret. He was visibly agitated during dinner.
suspects.private_info: You are innocent. Your agitation during dinner was entirely about O'Brien — you are furious that Valentin invited the man who once courted your daughter and then disgraced himself with debts. You despise O'Brien and consider him beneath your family. You stayed in the drawing room almost the entire evening, only stepping into the garden once briefly for air. You had no quarrel with Brayne — you found him vulgar but harmless. You will try to cast suspicion on O'Brien at every opportunity, not because you believe him guilty, but because you despise him.
suspects.alibi: Was in the drawing room for nearly the entire evening. Stepped into the garden only once, briefly, for air. His wife Lady Galloway can confirm his movements.
secrets: His fury at O'Brien is purely personal — about his daughter
He once tried to have O'Brien's commission revoked
He found Brayne crass but had no grievance with him
He saw O'Brien and Margaret speaking privately in the garden and is livid about it

suspects.name: Dr. Henri Simon
suspects.public_info: A typical French scientist, 48, with glasses, a pointed brown beard, and an air of intellectual superiority. He examined Brayne's body after the discovery and provided the initial medical assessment. He has a clinical, detached manner and seems more interested in the puzzle than the tragedy.
suspects.private_info: You are innocent. You are genuinely fascinated by the crime from a scientific perspective. Your examination of the body confirmed the decapitation was done with a single, powerful blow from a heavy, sharp blade — likely a sword or sabre. You noted that the cut was remarkably clean and precise, suggesting someone with training or great strength. You have no motive — you barely knew Brayne and had no personal connection to him. You were in the drawing room when the body was discovered. You will provide medical facts dispassionately and refuse to speculate beyond the evidence.
suspects.alibi: Was in the drawing room discussing philosophy with Father Brown for most of the evening. Did not enter the garden until called to examine the body.
secrets: His medical analysis points to a military-grade blade
He privately suspects O'Brien because of the sabre but has no proof
He noticed the weapons collection in the hall when he arrived
He found it odd that Valentin was so composed upon discovering the body

suspects.name: Father Brown
suspects.public_info: A small, round-faced priest from Cobhole in Essex, 45. Appears bumbling and innocuous — constantly misplacing his umbrella and blinking owlishly. He recently met Valentin in England. He has an unassuming manner that causes people to overlook him entirely. He seems an unlikely guest at such a distinguished gathering.
suspects.private_info: You are innocent — and you are far more perceptive than anyone realizes. You have been quietly observing everything all evening. You noticed several things others missed: Valentin seemed unusually tense before Brayne arrived. You saw Valentin pass through the entrance hall after the time of the murder, pausing near the weapon display. You noticed that one of the sabres on the wall appeared to have been recently handled — it hung at a slightly different angle than the others. You understand the impossible crime: the weapon didn't need to enter or leave because it was already there, on the wall. You won't accuse anyone directly unless pressed — you prefer to lead the detective to the truth through gentle questions. You are deeply sad about the whole affair.
suspects.alibi: Was in the drawing room most of the evening, speaking with Dr. Simon about theology and philosophy. Did not enter the garden until after the body was discovered.
secrets: Noticed Valentin lingering near the weapons wall after the murder
Observed that one sabre hangs at a different angle than the others
Understands the solution — the weapon was hiding in plain sight
Senses something deeply wrong about Valentin but pities him
Will hint at the truth rather than accuse directly

evidence_reveals.clue: Dr. Simon's medical examination confirms the decapitation was performed with a single blow from a heavy, sharp blade — consistent with a cavalry sabre or similar military sword. The cut is precise and clean, suggesting the wielder had significant strength or training. The body shows no defensive wounds — Brayne was likely struck from behind without warning. A thorough search of the garden reveals no weapon: no blade was found hidden in bushes, buried in soil, or thrown over the wall. The wall spikes show no disturbance. Ivan confirms no one carried any large object in or out through the front door.

Closer investigation reveals that Brayne was carrying a sealed letter in his jacket pocket, partially soaked with blood. The legible portions reference 'evidence that was manufactured' and 'an innocent man who still rots in prison.' The letter appears to be addressed to a Paris newspaper editor. Brayne had also made an appointment with a journalist for the following morning. Additionally, Commandant O'Brien's sabre has been examined — it is spotless, with no trace of blood, and its edge shows no signs of recent heavy use. Whatever blade killed Brayne, it was not O'Brien's.

A careful re-examination of the entrance hall reveals that the wall displays fourteen weapons — swords, sabres, pikes, and daggers collected over Valentin's career. One cavalry sabre, third from the left on the upper rack, hangs at a slightly different angle from the others, as if it was recently removed and replaced. When tested, this sabre shows microscopic traces of blood in the junction between blade and hilt — traces that survived a hasty cleaning. The blade edge is consistent with the wound profile Dr. Simon described. The weapon never left the house because it was always in the house — hanging on the wall in plain sight among a collection of similar weapons.

solution_data.motive: Brayne discovered Valentin fabricated evidence to convict an innocent man
solution_data.method: decapitation with cavalry sabre from his own wall collection — weapon hidden in plain sight

solution: Aristide Valentin, the Chief of the Paris Police and the host of the dinner party, murdered Julius K. Brayne by decapitating him with a cavalry sabre taken from his own weapons collection in the entrance hall. Brayne had discovered that Valentin — the great champion of justice — had fabricated evidence to convict an innocent man, and was preparing to expose him to the press. Valentin invited Brayne to dinner at his own home specifically because the sealed garden and guarded door would create an 'impossible' crime: no weapon could enter or leave. But the weapon was already inside, hanging on the wall. Valentin took the sabre from the hall, followed Brayne into the garden, struck him from behind with a single powerful blow, then returned inside, cleaned the blade, and hung it back in its place among the other weapons — hiding it in plain sight. The entrance hall was full of weapons; one more or less attracted no attention. Ivan, the loyal doorman, noticed nothing because the sabres had always been there. The crime was solved not by finding the weapon, but by realizing it had never been missing.
